EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought continued across north central New Mexico, however, drought conditions worsened, in some cases rapidly, across the south and east. Extreme (D3) drought conditions continued across the southwest mountains, and portions of west central New Mexico.  Extreme (D3) drought developed across portions of northeast and east central New Mexico early in the month, but rapidly increased to exceptional (D4) drought conditions later in the month in some areas.  Exceptional drought also developed much of central, south central, and southeast areas.   Most of these areas had received less than 20 percent of normal precipitation for the calendar year. The NOAA National Climatic Data Center reported that the first five months of 2011 had been the driest start to any year on record for New Mexico. Statewide precipitation was only 35 percent of normal.  As these drought conditions continued to worsen, human caused wildfires continued to pop up across portions of the state. Monetary damages will be estimated at the end of the event.

Drought conditions persisted and got worse of much of Southern New Mexico during the month of June as the entire area was in exceptional drought by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The northeastern corner of Dona Ana County started out in exceptional(D4) drought with the remainder of the area in extreme(D3) drought. By the end of June, the entire area was in exceptional(D4) drought except the far southeast. The COOP at New Mexico State University received 0.35 inches in the month. The yearly rainfall through the month of June at New Mexico State University was only 0.43 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted and got worse of much of Southern New Mexico during the month of June as the entire area was in exceptional drought by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire Southern Sacramento Mountains were in exceptional(D4) drought through the month of June. The Cloudcroft COOP received 0.68 inches in the month. The yearly rainfall through the month of June in Cloudcroft was 1.58 inches. Normal rainfall through the month of June is 9.18 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted and got worse of much of Southern New Mexico during the month of June as the entire area was in exceptional drought by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All of Otero County except the far southwest portion started out in exceptional(D4) drought and remained there through the month. The Alamogordo airport only received 0.03 inches of precipitation in the month. The yearly rainfall through the month of June at the Alamogordo airport was only 0.09 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough was moving onshore while a strong ridge was located over the eastern United States. This setup a moist southerly flow at mid-levels and a dry line was located over the Rio Grande Valley, allowing for severe thunderstorms to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58 mph wind gust was reported 4 miles west of Organ at a New Mexico Environment Department Mesonet site.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS ABOVE 9500 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Pacheco wildfire was a human caused wildfire that began two miles north of the Santa Fe Ski Basin during the afternoon of the 18th.  This fire continued into July before it was contained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pacheco fire scorched 10,250 acres in all.  Though no buildings were damaged, 12 power poles burned during the event.  This power line fed both the Tesuque Peak NOAA weather radio transmitter as well as the Santa Fe Ski Basin. After some communication interruption, generators were installed to facilitate operations.  Total damage estimates will be entered at the end of the event.

A Pacific cold front moved across west Texas and southeast New Mexico during the overnight hours.  Increasing low level moisture convergence along this front and a dryline combined with an approaching mid level shortwave trough to produce severe thunderstorms from the southeast New Mexico plains through portions of the Permian Basin.  Steep lapse rates and modest deep layer shear supported large hail.

A strong upper low dropped down the Northern Rockies which pushed an arctic back door cold front through the region. Over a foot of snow fell in the Sacramento Mountains with lesser amounts over the Gila and lowlands of Southern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports across the area included 9.5 inches 2.3 miles east-northeast of Alamogordo, 9.0 inches 4.6 miles north of Alamogordo, 6.5 inches 2.1 miles southeast of Alamogordo, and 6.5 inches 1 mile east of Tularosa.

A deep upper low was diving down the west coast while a surface low rapidly deepened over the Texas and Oklahoma panhandle area. A tight pressure gradient setup across the region with strong southwest winds ahead of the upper low.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gust of 89 mph was reported at the San Augustin Pass RAWS. Other peak gusts across the area included 75 mph at Salinas Peak, 58 mph at the National Weather Service office in Santa Teresa and 61 mph at the White Sands Missile Range Main Post.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gust of 65 mph was reported at Northrup Strip on the White Sands Missile Range.

Isolated severe storms formed across the eastern plains of New Mexico during the afternoon and evening of the 10th. Unfortunately, due to there location, verifying reports were few and far between. It's likely that greater than quarter size hail fell over Johnson Mesa as the storm grew in size and intensity after the nickel hail report.  Earlier in the afternoon, a slow moving severe storm tracked across De Baca County, producing severe wind gusts which downed tree limbs.

Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across much of New Mexico on the 2nd.  This thunderstorm developed just west of Raton and slowly moved east, briefly becoming severe over the city limits.

Though drier air was trying to keep its grasp on the northern portion of the state, a very moist and unstable airmass remained across the southern half of New Mexico. A strong thunderstorm with very heavy rain moved squarely over the White Fire burn scar dumping up to 2 inches per radar estimates.  A few hours later, a seemingly typical afternoon thunderstorm north of Clovis produced violent wind gusts as the storm quickly collapsed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A KVII Schoolnet site north of Clovis recorded winds from a wet microburst. Winds initially were from the north-northeast at 72 mph at 545 pm MST, then increased to 79 mph at 549 pm.  The wind direction switched to the south-southwest at 652 pm and a final gust of 64 mph occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains on the eastern half of the White Fire burn scar led to flash flooding along the Rio Ruidoso downstream toward Glencoe and Hondo.  Radar estimates near 3 inches of rain fell in this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported the Rio Ruidoso out of its banks for a 6 to 10 mile stretch downstream of the White Fire burn scar.  High water levels in the canyons and arroyos on the east and south side of the burn scar flowed into the Rio Ruidoso, causing it to flood.  Debris from the burn area was piled up on the banks. Near mile marker 272.5 on Highway 70, portions of the Rio de Oso Trailer park was underwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal precipitation in August thanks to monsoonal showers and thunderstorms allowed the west central plateau to drop out of the severe (D2) rating by the end of the month.

A weak monsoon pattern was in place with a upper high over central Texas and a southeast surface flow into south-central New Mexico. There was a weak boundary that extended through Luna County into eastern Arizona which was the focus for thunderstorms. Very weak flow of less than 5 knots which limited storm motion and allowed for heavy rain to occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Staff at Deming Aerostat reported water flowing across Hermanas Road.  Over an inch of rain fell in a short period of time at the location according to Doppler Radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface low was located near Las Cruces with very little flow aloft allowed very slow moving thunderstorms to develop across the area. Very heavy rain occurred over Las Cruces along with localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New Mexico State University police reported a large amount of standing water making for hazardous travel on the eastbound lane of College between Knox and Union.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level disturbance crossed New Mexico, helping create strong surface winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 mph was recorded at 853 am MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two new fires started during the afternoon of the 17th across New Mexico.  A small fire near Alcalde burned two abandoned structures, while a larger fire south of Melrose, scorched over 20,000 acres and destroyed three homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several media outlets as well as the Southwest Coordination Center reported that a grass fire erupted during the late morning hours of the 17th. A person traveling along Highway 267 had a blow out which caused sparks along the rim of the vehicle, igniting the fire.  The Tire Fire raced eastward from six miles south of Melrose eastward along the Curry/Roosevelt county line for approximately 20 miles as antecedent dry conditions and east winds of at least 20 mph fueled the fire. In all, 24,000 acres of land was scorched, much of it cattle grazing land, and three homes were destroyed.  No cattle were harmed, however, three firefighters were treated for minor burns and smoke inhalation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very little if any rain fell across Far Southern New Mexico since the beginning of the year which led to extreme to exceptional drought conditions across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of May, the entire area was under Extreme(D3) drought. By the end of the month, the far northeast corner of Dona Ana County was into Exceptional(D4) drought. No precipitation fell in the month of May and the Las Cruces Airport had no received any precipitation since early February.

As an upper level storm system approached the state, very strong mid level winds overspread the area. With daytime heating, these strong winds mixed down to the surface resulting in gusts near or over 60 mph in many areas across western New Mexico as well as along and just downstream of the central mountain chain. Areas of blowing dust reduced visibilities below 3 miles at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For nearly six hours, the AWOS at Sierra Blanca Regional Airport reported gusts at or above 58 mph.  The peak gust of 70 mph occurred at 1215 pm.

With daytime heating, these strong winds mixed down to the surface resulting in gusts near or over 60 mph in many areas across western New Mexico as well as along and just downstream of the central mountain chain. Areas of blowing dust reduced visibilities below 3 miles at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 40 mph were reported at Las Vegas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper level storm system approached the state, very strong mid level winds overspread the area. With daytime heating, these strong winds mixed down to the surface resulting in gusts near or over 60 mph in many areas across western New Mexico as well as along and just downstream of the central mountain chain. Areas of blowing dust reduced visibilities below 3 miles at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Columbia Scientific Balloon Facilities Transwestern Pump Station north-northeast of Encinoso recorded a few wind gusts over 58 mph.  The strongest wind gust was 62 mph which occurred at 225 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper level storm system approached the state, very strong mid level winds overspread the area. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Magdalena Ridge Observatory recorded a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought developed across much of central New Mexico late in March after the spring winds began to pick up and most areas received less than 10 percent of the normal precipitation for the month. Winter precipitation varied across central portions of the state, ranging from around 40 percent to near 120 percent around Albuquerque and Moriarty. These drought conditions are expected to worsen, setting the stage for wildfires. Monetary damages will be estimated at the end of the event.

A powerful storm and arctic cold front combined to bring fresh snow cover to nearly all of northern and central New Mexico on the 1st and 2nd, as well as extremely cold, record setting minimum temperatures. This resulted in dangerously low wind chill values over many areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures down to -35 degrees and 5 mph winds created wind chill values of -35 to -45 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some monsoon moisture gave a reprieve to the ongoing drought conditions, but just about all areas of Southern New Mexico remained at least in Extreme(D3) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All of Luna County was in Exceptional(D4) drought at the beginning of August with the whole county at Extreme(D3) drought at the end of the month. The Deming Airport reported 1.29 inches of rain in the month which brought the yearly total up to 2.32 inches. Normal precipitation through August at Deming is just over 6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some monsoon moisture gave a reprieve to the ongoing drought conditions, but just about all areas of Southern New Mexico remained at least in Extreme(D3) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire area was in Exceptional(D4) drought at the beginning of the month and all areas except the far eastern part of the bootheel, which was in Extreme(D3) drought, remained in the Exceptional(D4) category. The cooperative observer in Antelope Wells received 1.86 inches of rain in the month which brought the yearly total up to 3.74 inches. Normal yearly precipitation through August is almost 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some monsoon moisture gave a reprieve to the ongoing drought conditions, but just about all areas of Southern New Mexico remained at least in Extreme(D3) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire area was in Exceptional(D4) drought at the beginning of August and was in Extreme(D3) drought at the end of the month. The cooperative observer at Redrock reported 0.93 inches or rain during the month which brought the yearly total up to 2.35 inches. The normal yearly precipitation through August is over 8.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some monsoon moisture gave a reprieve to the ongoing drought conditions, but just about all areas of Southern New Mexico remained at least in Extreme(D3) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Except for the far southwestern portions of Otero County which was in Extreme(D3) drought, the remainder of the Otero County plains were in Exceptional(D4) drought to start the month. By the end of August, the Extreme(D3) conditions extended from the far southwest corner of the county up through extreme western Otero County. The remainder of the area remained in Exceptional(D4) drought. The Alamogordo Airport reported only 0.19 inches of rain for the month which brought the yearly total up to 0.82 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought in the southwest mountains began in late January of 2011 and in the east central plains in late February of 2011. After below normal winter precipitation, March 2011 did not help matters. In fact, much of Chaves, Roosevelt and Curry counties received no precipitation. Precipitation amounts in the southwest mountains were less than 20 percent of normal for March. These drought conditions are expected to worsen, setting the stage for wildfires. Monetary damages will be estimated at the end of the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that formed over the Sangre de Cristo Mountains early in the afternoon moved northeast onto the lower terrain where low level moisture was considerably less.  As the storms diminished, very strong outflow boundaries moved across the Raton Crews Airport.  Later in the afternoon, another strong outflow boundary impacted Grants.  Though the thunderstorm activity quieted down in the evening, a rogue severe storm impacted Clayton, producing yet another round of severe winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Outflow boundaries with winds exceeding 58 mph continuously impacted the Raton Crews Airport.

Severe (D2) drought began across the south central mountains and adjacent highlands, lower Rio Grande Valley as well as portions of northeast New Mexico in late March. Winter precipitation was better across the northeast, but many areas were still below normal. Isolated areas received decent precipitation in March, but much of the area received less than 40 percent of normal precipitation. These drought conditions are expected to worsen, setting the stage for wildfires. Monetary damages will be estimated at the end of the event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Las Vegas ASOS recorded severe wind gusts of 58 and 59 mph several times during the four hour period.  The highest gust of 59 mph was recorded at 926 am.

A wildfire developed in Curry County, damaging one structure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire scorched a 7 mile long by 1/2 mile wide path from Curry Road H near Ned Houk Memorial Park to Curry Road D.  One home was damaged in the area of Curry Road G and 19.  In all, the wildfire burned 4000 acres. This wildfire continued to burn into March.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third exposure death in the Farmington area of the winter season occurred during the morning of the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 61 year-old man died of exposure in a wooded area near the intersection of Southside River Road and River View Lane in Farmington.  Morning low temperatures only dipped down to 31 degrees in Farmington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought in the southwest mountains began in late January due to a poor showing of winter precipitation. These drought conditions are expected to worsen, setting the stage for wildfires. Monetary damages will be estimated at the end of the event.

Thunderstorms over southeast and east central New Mexico managed to produce isolated strong wind gusts that blew a roof off a barn north of Dexter in Chaves County and downed trees and large branches in Portales.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof of a barn collapsed and killed an elderly man.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled boundary combined with abundant moisture and instability led to quick thunderstorm development across northeast New Mexico on the 28th. Rainfall rates exceeding two inches per hour were reported. Slow and erratic storm motions increased the flash flooding potential over the area.  Areas over the Track Fire burn scar as well as in Folsom were the hardest hit.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant runoff transported considerable amounts of ash and debris from the Track Fire burn scar into Lake Maloya. All lakes and ponds in the Sugarite area were bankfull.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hail and heavy rains plagued the Sandia and Manzano Mountains on the 26th.  Fortunately though, despite some ponding of water in a parking lot, no flooding was reported.  The worst of it was nickel sized hail near Ponderosa.
Some monsoon moisture gave a reprieve to the ongoing drought conditions, but just about all areas of Southern New Mexico remained at least in Extreme(D3) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All of Dona Ana County was in Exceptional(D4) drought except for the far southeast corner which was mainly in Extreme(D3) drought to start the month. At the end of the month, all the areas that were in Exceptional drought were in Extreme(D3) drought with the extreme southeast corner of the county in Severe(D2) drought. The cooperative observer at New Mexico State University received 1.38 inches of rain in August which brought the yearly total up to 3.82 inches.  The normal yearly precipitation through August is around 6 inches.

Drought conditions started to ease a bit across Southern New Mexico during the month of November as a couple of storms brought significant rain, especially to the Bootheel and Gila regions. The month started out with far Southwest New Mexico and Eastern Otero County in Exceptional(D4) drought which was dropped to Extreme(D3) drought by the end of the month. Most of the remainder of the area was in Extreme(D3) drought  through the entire month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire zone started out November in Exceptional(D4) drought. By the end of the month, the entire area was in Extreme(D3) drought. The COOP at Antelope Wells received 3.91 inches of rain for the month which was over 3 inches above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions started to ease a bit across Southern New Mexico during the month of November as a couple of storms brought significant rain, especially to the Bootheel and Gila regions. The month started out with far Southwest New Mexico and Eastern Otero County in Exceptional(D4) drought which was dropped to Extreme(D3) drought by the end of the month. Most of the remainder of the area was in Extreme(D3) drought  through the entire month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme southwest corner of the zone started out in Exceptional(D4) drought with the remainder of the zone in Extreme(D3) drought. At the end of the month there was little change across the area. The COOP observer at Faywood received 1.70 inches of rain which was over an inch above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions continued across Southern New Mexico through July with Exceptional Drought(D4) conditions across ninety five percent of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exceptional Drought(D4) conditions continued across all of the Tularosa Basin and southern desert of Otero County through the month of July except for a small area over far Southwest Otero County which was in Extreme Drought(D3). The Alamogordo Airport did receive 0.54 inches of rain in the month, but this only brought the yearly total up to 0.63 inches. The average annual precipitation is around 13 inches. The cooperative observer in Orogrande did receive 4.11 inches for the month, but only brought the yearly total up to 4.20 inches.  The yearly average precipitation is around 12 inches in Orogrande.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions started to ease a bit across Southern New Mexico during the month of November as a couple of storms brought significant rain, especially to the Bootheel and Gila regions. Most of the remainder of the area was in Extreme(D3) drought  through the entire month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Initial drought conditions ranged from Exceptional(D4) drought over the southeast portion of the zone to Severe(D2) drought over the southwest corner. There was little change during the month of November. The COOP observer in Orogrande received just 0.23 inches of rain which was 0.23 inches below normal.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire zone remained in Extreme(D3) drought during the month of November. New Mexico State University received 0.42 inches of rain which was near normal for the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions started to ease a bit across Southern New Mexico during the month of November as a couple of storms brought significant rain, especially to the Bootheel and Gila regions. The month started out with far Southwest New Mexico and Eastern Otero County in Exceptional(D4) drought which was dropped to Extreme(D3) drought by the end of the month. Most of the remainder of the area was in Extreme(D3) drought  through the entire month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southwest portion of Luna County started the month in Exceptional(D4) drought with the remainder of the county in Extreme(D3) drought. At the end of the month, the entire zone was in Extreme(D3) drought. The Deming ASOS recorded 1.55 inches of rain for the month which was 0.84 inches above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions started to ease a bit across Southern New Mexico during the month of November as a couple of storms brought significant rain, especially to the Bootheel and Gila regions. The month started out with far Southwest New Mexico and Eastern Otero County in Exceptional(D4) drought which was dropped to Extreme(D3) drought by the end of the month. Most of the remainder of the area was in Extreme(D3) drought  through the entire month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All of the Southern Sacramento Mountains were in Exceptional(D4) drought to start the month. By the end of November, only the extreme southeast section of the mountains were still in Exceptional(D4) drought and the remainder of the area was in Extreme(D3) drought. The Cloudcroft COOP Observer received 1.34 inches of rain for the month which was just 0.10 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: All of Southwest and South Central New Mexico remained in Severe to Exceptional Drought through the month of October. There was some small improvement in conditions across the Gila. Near to slightly below normal rainfall was experienced across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire zone remained in extreme(D3) drought through the month of October.  The Truth or Consequences Airport received 0.62 inches of rain which was 0.30 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: All of Southwest and South Central New Mexico remained in Severe to Exceptional Drought through the month of October. There was some small improvement in conditions across the Gila. Near to slightly below normal rainfall was experienced across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southeastern corner of the zone was in Severe(D2) drought through the month while the remainder of the zone was in Extreme(D3) drought. The official COOP at New Mexico State University received 0.05 inches of rain while the average for October is 0.78 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: All of Southwest and South Central New Mexico remained in Severe to Exceptional Drought through the month of October. There was some small improvement in conditions across the Gila. Near to slightly below normal rainfall was experienced across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern Sacramento Mountains remained in Exceptional(D4) drought through the entire month of October. The COOP in Cloudcroft received 1.43 inches of rain which was 0.45 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped focus thunderstorm development over northeast New Mexico with a few reports of severe weather, including large hail and high wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail size ranged from peas to quarters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped focus thunderstorm development over northeast New Mexico with a few reports of severe weather, including large hail and high wind gusts.

An upper ridge was centered over Central Texas with a moist southerly flow setup over the region. Training thunderstorms developed over the Sacramento Mountains and dropped over five inches of rain around Cloudcroft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 5.5 inches of rain was reported in Cloudcroft. Flooding was reported around the Cloudcroft High School area, around the water treatment plant and over Highway 130. The intersection of Chautauqua and Woodlands Roads was closed due to water over the road. Other rainfall totals over the Sacramento Mountains included 2.99 inches 4 miles southwest of Silver Lake and 2.65 inches of rain at the Dry Canyon Mesonet Station 3 miles south-southwest of Silver Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low was setup near the bootheel of New Mexico with southeast winds over far South-Central New Mexico and a southerly flow aloft. This allowed for a brief landspout tornado to occur near the Santa Teresa Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service Employees saw a landspout tornado about 3 miles west of the office with some dust debris on the ground. The tornado did not strike any structures and remained in the rural desert.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper high was centered near the Texas Panhandle with a deep, moist easterly flow in place across the Gila Region. Several thunderstorm clusters produced flash flooding in several parts of Grant County where a couple of locations reported nearly three inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along Cottage San Road just north of Silver City. A rainfall total of 2.67 inches was reported 4.4 miles north of Silver City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper high was centered near the Texas Panhandle with a deep, moist easterly flow in place across the Gila Region. Several thunderstorm clusters produced flash flooding in several parts of Grant County where a couple of locations reported nearly three inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was crossing Racetrack Road and Fellner Street in Santa Clara. The water was coming out of Twin Sisters and Cameron Creeks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper high was centered over Texas with a south-southwest flow aloft bringing abundant moisture to Western New Mexico. Training storms brought heavy rainfall to the far northwest corner of Sierra County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Poverty Creek was overflowing its banks and water was crossing Highway 59 in Poverty Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions continued across Southern New Mexico through July with Exceptional Drought(D4) conditions across ninety five percent of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of Dona Ana County was in Exceptional Drought(D4) through the month of July except for the far southeast portion of the county which was in Extreme Drought(D3). |The official observing site at New Mexico State University did receive 2.01 inches of precipitation in the month, but since early October 2010, the total precipitation was only 2.45 inches.  The average yearly precipitation at this site is over 9.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions continued across Southern New Mexico through July with Exceptional Drought(D4) conditions across ninety five percent of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exceptional Drought(D4) conditions continued across all of the Sacramento Mountains through the month of July. The cooperative observer at Mountain Park only received 2.67 inches of rain in the month and the observer at Elk only received 0.79 inches with the yearly total only at 1.42 inches at the end of the month. The average annual precipitation at Elk is over 17 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions continued across Southern New Mexico through July with Exceptional Drought(D4) conditions across ninety five percent of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exceptional Drought(D4) conditions continued across all of Luna County through the month of July. The Deming Airport did receive 1.00 inches of rain in the month, but this only brought the yearly total up to 1.03 inches. The average annual precipitation is around 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions continued across Southern New Mexico through July with Exceptional Drought(D4) conditions across ninety five percent of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exceptional Drought(D4) conditions continued across all of the Gila Region through the month of July. The Silver City Airport did receive 1.78 inches of rain in the month, but this only brought the yearly total up to 1.88 inches. The cooperative observer at Redrock received 1.18 inches of rain in the month but only brought the yearly total to 1.42 inches.  The average annual precipitation at Redrock is over 13 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropping south across the eastern plains and an approaching storm system from the west combined to create widespread showers and thunderstorms over eastern New Mexico. One of these storms became severe in Curry County, producing large hail.
Very light shower activity stretching across much of the southeastern plains, resulted in a brief wind gust at the Roswell Airport.

Scattered showers and thunderstorms over southeast New Mexico brought hail, strong winds and funnel clouds to the area. Fortunately, the hail was sub-severe levels and the funnel clouds never developed into tornadoes.  While the hail occurred in De Baca County, multiple funnel clouds were reported in southern Chaves County.

All of Southwest and South Central New Mexico remained in Severe to Exceptional Drought through the month of October. There was some small improvement in conditions across the Gila. Near to slightly below normal rainfall was experienced across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The western third of Luna County was in Exceptional(D4) drought through the month while the eastern two-thirds of the county was in Extreme(D3) drought. The Deming Airport received 0.71 inches of rain which was 0.14 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: All of Southwest and South Central New Mexico remained in Severe to Exceptional Drought through the month of October. There was some small improvement in conditions across the Gila. Near to slightly below normal rainfall was experienced across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire zone was in Exceptional(D4) drought through the month. The Antelope Wells COOP received 0.61 inches of rain which was just 0.07 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions continued across Southern New Mexico through July with Exceptional Drought(D4) conditions across ninety five percent of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exceptional Drought(D4) conditions continued across all of Hidalgo County through the month of July. The cooperative observer at Antelope Wells did receive 1.88 inches of rain in the month, but this was the first measurable precipitation since December 2010. The average annual precipitation at Antelope Wells is 12.38 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: November was dry for much of the state.  Many areas across eastern New Mexico received less than 15 percent of normal precipitation for the month.  Northern and western areas fared better, with many areas near or just above normal precipitation. Percent of normal precipitation for the autumn months trended the same way; much of the eastern plains were 50 to 75 percent of normal, while the northwest portions of the state were the wettest at 100 to 150 percent of normal. Therefore, drought improved very little over the month of August.  Some  northwest areas dropped a drought category but much of the state remained in severe (D2) or greater drought status.  The east central and southeast plains remained in exceptional (D4) drought.  Monetary damages will be estimated at the end of the event.

A strong upper level low pressure system that slowly moved south over Arizona and then over southern New Mexico brought copious amounts of snow to the state.  Some of the areas that were hit the hardest included the same areas that were blanketed by snow from the previous storm.  However, this time, it was the southeast portion of the state, rather than the northeast portion of the state, that was hit hard. Another cold front moved down the plains and through the gaps of the central mountain chain on the 22nd, and snow quickly developed across the western two-thirds of the state. One to two feet of snow was common across the east slopes of the Sandia and Manzano Mountains as well as the west central and southwest mountains.  As the low slowly moved east across southern portions of the state, snow was heaviest from the south central mountains eastward to the Texas state line.  These areas saw between 6 and 12 inches of snow at lower elevations, while well over a foot of fresh snow was reported across the high terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 18 inches of snow fell across the Sandia and Manzano Mountains.  This amount was reported at the Sandia Ski Area, Sedillo, near Manzano and just east of Tijeras.

A tight surface pressure gradient and low level jet behind a strong cold front resulted in high winds across southeast New Mexico.

A strong upper trough moved across the region and combined with an area of high pressure over the northern plains which pushed a strong cold front through the region. Snow levels fell to the valley floors across Southern New Mexico with snowfall totals of up to 7 inches reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The maximum snowfall across the Gila region was 4.8 inches 3 miles southwest of Silver City. A spotter 3 miles north-northeast of Tyrone reported 4.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cutoff low moved across extreme Southern New Mexico and into the Texas Panhandle bringing heavy snows to portions of the Gila and Sacramento Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total snowfall in Cloudcroft was 6.5 inches and 6.0 inches was reported 2 miles southwest of Cloudcroft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cutoff low moved across extreme Southern New Mexico and into the Texas Panhandle bringing heavy snows to portions of the Gila and Sacramento Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter 2 miles west-southwest of White Signal reported 4.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought severe winds to New Mexico early on the 5th. As mid level wind speeds strengthened to 50 to 70 knots, a surface cold front barreled across the state from west to east.  As the front crossed the central mountain chain and moved across the eastern plains, it's assumed that some mountain wave activity broke onto the surface which caused wind speeds to escalate into the damaging wind category.   Though winds were strong behind the front to the Texas border, wind gusts were strongest just to the lee of the central mountain chain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Roswell ASOS recorded at 58 mph wind gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dunken RAWS site recorded a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: As enhanced radar echoes moved over the KSKX AWOS, some mid level momentum was transported to the surface, resulting in an 82 mph wind gust.  No damage was reported by airport personnel.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave trough crossed over the central Rocky Mountains during the overnight hours. Mid level winds in the base of the trough were approximately 70 to 80 knots.  This resulted in very strong winds over the high terrain of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains.  Mountain waves crashing over the adjacent highlands brought periodic high winds downstream as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Angel Fire AWOS measured wind gusts exceeding 58 mph for at least 40 minutes. The peak wind gust of 64 mph occurred at 1255 am MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave trough crossed over the central Rocky Mountains during the overnight hours. Mid level winds in the base of the trough were approximately 70 to 80 knots.  This resulted in very strong winds over the high terrain of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains.  Mountain waves crashing over the adjacent highlands brought periodic high winds downstream as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mountain wave crashed onto the Raton ASOS causing a gust to 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a storm system that brought some much needed rain to portions of the state, a back door cold front slammed through the eastern plains of New Mexico during the early morning hours of the 26th. This caused high temperatures to drop 20 degrees from the previous day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clayton ASOS measured sustained winds of 40 mph for at least one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a storm system that brought some much needed rain to portions of the state, a back door cold front slammed through the eastern plains of New Mexico during the early morning hours of the 26th. This caused high temperatures to drop 20 degrees from the previous day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clovis AWOS measured sustained winds of at least 40 mph for one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper low moved from Southeast Arizona through far Northern Mexico and into Southeast New Mexico. A surface cold front pushed well south of the border allowing for cold air to penetrate into the region. Up to 24 inches of snow was reported in the Sacramento Mountains with this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP Observer in Sacramento reported a storm total snowfall of 24.0 inches. Other reports included 17.1 inches 5 miles northeast of Cloudcroft, 14.8 inches in Cloudcroft, 12.9 inches 3 miles southwest of Silver Lake and 9.0 inches 1 mile south of Pinon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system that slowly moved south over Arizona and then over southern New Mexico brought copious amounts of snow to the state.  Some of the areas that were hit the hardest included the same areas that were blanketed by snow from the previous storm.  However, this time, it was the southeast portion of the state, rather than the northeast portion of the state, that was hit hard. Another cold front moved down the plains and through the gaps of the central mountain chain on the 22nd, and snow quickly developed across the western two-thirds of the state. One to two feet of snow was common across the east slopes of the Sandia and Manzano Mountains as well as the west central and southwest mountains.  As the low slowly moved east across southern portions of the state, snow was heaviest from the south central mountains eastward to the Texas state line.  These areas saw between 6 and 12 inches of snow at lower elevations, while well over a foot of fresh snow was reported across the high terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 5 inches of snow fell just southeast of Clines Corners, but nearly 8 inches of snow fell at Duran.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought severe winds to New Mexico early on the 5th. As mid level wind speeds strengthened to 50 to 70 knots, a surface cold front barreled across the state from west to east.  As the front crossed the central mountain chain and moved across the eastern plains, it's assumed that some mountain wave activity broke onto the surface which caused wind speeds to escalate into the damaging wind category.   Though winds were strong behind the front to the Texas border, wind gusts were strongest just to the lee of the central mountain chain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Las Vegas ASOS recorded intermittent wind gusts over 58 mph for over 2 hours.  The peak wind gust was 66 mph which occurred at 1204 pm MST.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES OF THE SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper level closed low skirted across northern New Mexico, a back door cold front squeaked into the northeast. While snow showers were scattered across much of northern and western New Mexico, snow amounts were enhanced in favored upslope regions of the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains. This quick-hitting storm system left over a foot of snow in some of the high peaks of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas near Arroyo Seco received 8.5 inches of snow.  Elsewhere, amounts were closer to 4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Almost all of Southwest and South-Central New Mexico started the month in Extreme(D3) to Exceptional(D4) drought. By the end of the month, with the exception of the eastern half of Otero County, every other area was downgraded to Moderate(D1) to Severe(D2) drought due to an unusually wet month across Southwest New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire zone started out in Extreme(D3) drought and was downgraded to Severe(D2) drought by the end of the month. Rainfall at the Truth or Consequences Airport was 2.85 inches which was 2.40 inches above normal.

Some of the areas that were hit the hardest included the same areas that were blanketed by snow from the previous storm.  However, this time, it was the southeast portion of the state, rather than the northeast portion of the state, that was hit hard. Another cold front moved down the plains and through the gaps of the central mountain chain on the 22nd, and snow quickly developed across the western two-thirds of the state. One to two feet of snow was common across the east slopes of the Sandia and Manzano Mountains as well as the west central and southwest mountains.  As the low slowly moved east across southern portions of the state, snow was heaviest from the south central mountains eastward to the Texas state line.  These areas saw between 6 and 12 inches of snow at lower elevations, while well over a foot of fresh snow was reported across the high terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near Fence Lake, approximately 6 inches of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front plowed down the eastern plains of New Mexico early on the 2nd.  Though gusty winds were noted area wide, the strongest wind gust occurred at Raton, perhaps due to winds funneling through Raton Pass.

A strong storm system brought severe winds to New Mexico early on the 5th. As mid level wind speeds strengthened to 50 to 70 knots, a surface cold front barreled across the state from west to east.  As the front crossed the central mountain chain and moved across the eastern plains, it's assumed that some mountain wave activity broke onto the surface which caused wind speeds to escalate into the damaging wind category.   Though winds were strong behind the front to the Texas border, wind gusts were strongest just to the lee of the central mountain chain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in Stanley reported a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two successive upper level storm systems effectively brought a one-two punch to New Mexico.  Winter weather began on the 31st of January when an arctic cold front blasted through the eastern plains.  Snow began that evening in earnest across the eastern plains and central mountain chain.  Rain also changed to snow across the west as temperatures plummeted. Snow continued into the 2nd, which was followed by extremely cold temperatures area wide that not only shattered daily records on the 3rd but at some locales broke the all-time coldest temperatures on record.  It was the most significant outbreak of arctic air since January 1941.  Snow totals exceeded one foot in many mountain locales, while somewhat lower amounts were noted below 7500 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 8 inches of snow fell near and in Magdalena.  Elsewhere, 3 to 6 inch amounts were common.

Winter weather began on the 31st of January when an arctic cold front blasted through the eastern plains.  Snow began that evening in earnest across the eastern plains and central mountain chain.  Rain also changed to snow across the west as temperatures plummeted. Snow continued into the 2nd, which was followed by extremely cold temperatures area wide that not only shattered daily records on the 3rd but at some locales broke the all-time coldest temperatures on record.  It was the most significant outbreak of arctic air since January 1941.  Snow totals exceeded one foot in many mountain locales, while somewhat lower amounts were noted below 7500 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the east side of Albuquerque, 4 to 6 inches of snow was common.  West of Interstate 25, amounts were generally 1 to 2 inches.  Three to 6 inches was also common around Belen, Los Lunas and Bosque Farms.  Spotters also reported hearing thunder around Albuquerque.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold late season upper level low pressure system over the northern Rockies developed southward into the Four Corners region on May 1st.  A potent cold front also moved south and west across the eastern plains providing support for strong easterly upslope flow along the central mountain chain. Widespread snowfall between 6 and 10 inches was reported over the northern mountains, with lighter amounts of 2 to 6 inches of the surrounding highlands and upper Rio Grande Valley.  Strong gap winds within the central valley also delivered wind gusts between 40 and 60 mph during the early morning hours May 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various snowfall amounts between 4 and 8 inches were reported from public and spotters. Wagon Mound received 8 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After the precipitation ended, at least significantly so, on the 1st,  brutally cold temperatures and strong winds impacted eastern New Mexico.  Wind chill values of -25 to -40 degrees Fahrenheit were reported across much of northeast and east central New Mexico during the overnight.  Actual low temperatures ranged from 0 to 15 below zero in these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures around -14F combined with northerly winds of 10 to 20 mph with higher gusts resulted in early morning wind chill values from 25 to 40 degrees below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As snow changed to rain across central New Mexico, much of the precipitation struggled to reach the ground due to drier near-surface conditions.  However, the resultant virga created localized but very strong wind gusts stretching from Santa Fe to Albuquerque and Moriarty.  No lightning occurred with these virga showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Albuquerque ASOS recorded a peak wind gust of 77 mph.  Blowing dust reduced visibility below 2 miles for a time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air overspread the region on 2/1/2011.  Northeast winds of 30-45 mph with higher gusts combined with temperatures in the single digits to produce wind chill values of -15 to -35 degrees Fahrenheit.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tatum West Texas Mesonet site indicated wind chills of -15 to -25 degrees Fahrenheit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low dropped down the Northern Rockies which pushed a back door arctic cold front through the region. An upper level ridge was located over the eastern Pacific with the arctic jet steam diving south to the Mexican border.  These two features combined to bring extremely cold air into the Western United States from Alaska and Canada. Numerous areas experienced rolling power outages and natural gas shortages along with burst pipes. The state of New Mexico declared a State of Emergency due to the natural gas shortages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures across the region on Feb 2nd included 9F at McGregor Range, 16 at Alamogordo and 18 at Orogrande. Low temperatures on the morning of Feb 3rd included -19F at Holloman Air Force Base, -13F at Alamogordo Airport, -11F at Orogrande and -7F at McGregor Range. Six water main breaks were reported in Alamogordo.  Natural gas was shutoff to about 5000 customers in the area. The city of Alamogordo declared an emergency in the city due to the extreme winter weather. Around 4000 residents in the county were without natural gas for several days. Most schools were closed for 3 days due to the cold and lack of water and heat, a couple in Alamogordo were closed for 5 days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two new fires started during the afternoon of the 17th across New Mexico.  A small fire near Alcalde burned two abandoned structures, while a larger fire south of Melrose, scorched over 20,000 acres and destroyed three homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small 50-acre fire near Alcalde, NM, named the Palm Fire, burned two abandoned structures.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Artesia AWOS indicated wind chills of -15 to-20 degrees Fahrenheit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two successive upper level storm systems effectively brought a one-two punch to New Mexico.  Winter weather began on the 31st of January when an arctic cold front blasted through the eastern plains.  Snow began that evening in earnest across the eastern plains and central mountain chain.  Rain also changed to snow across the west as temperatures plummeted. Snow continued into the 2nd, which was followed by extremely cold temperatures area wide that not only shattered daily records on the 3rd but at some locales broke the all-time coldest temperatures on record.  It was the most significant outbreak of arctic air since January 1941.  Snow totals exceeded one foot in many mountain locales, while somewhat lower amounts were noted below 7500 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of snow fell around Crownpoint.

A broad upper level trough swung over New Mexico on the 18th and 19th.  While the trough brought some much needed precipitation to the northwest, it also generated strong winds statewide.  Dry low levels resulted in very gusty winds in isolated locales as precipitation largely evaporated.  In other areas, the gusty winds were the result of mountain waves crashing onto the surface.  Regardless of the cause, winds topped 60 mph across portions of northern and central New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a mountain wave crashed onto the surface, the Clines Corners ASOS measured a 62 mph wind gust.

Antecedent dry conditions combined with strong winds and low humidities fueled this human caused fire in the Gila Wilderness.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This human caused wildfire, named the Miller Fire, began at the end of April and continued well into June, burning within the steep and rugged terrain of the Gila Wilderness.  Four, generally unused, trailers burned near the Gila Cliff Dwellings Visitor in May, but fortunately, all of the historic cabins were saved. 13 minor injuries occurred with the fire fighting efforts, which are also believed to have occurred in May.  In total, 88,835 acres burned, though less than half of this area was within Catron County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds fueled the Milagro Fire, especially on the 8th and 9th. Very dry antecedent conditions allowed this fire to quickly spread through western Guadalupe County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This human caused fire burned 6159 acres just north of Interstate 40, north of Milagro.  This so-called Milagro Fire destroyed one outbuilding and damaged another.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Aptly named the Windy Fire, this blaze quickly scorched over 30,000 acres under very windy conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Windy Fire originated 3 miles east of McAlister and quickly spread under very windy conditions along the Curry and Quay county line. One unoccupied residence was burned as was 6 outbuildings. In all, 32,143 acres were scorched.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a storm system approached New Mexico on the 3rd, the mid level gradient and surface pressure gradient increased sharply, leading to high, damaging winds.  These strong winds fueled a new wildfire near Ruidoso.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred between 1230 pm and 350 pm MST around the Albuquerque Metro Area. The Double Eagle Airport reported a peak wind gust of 66 mph at 150 pm MST, with sustained winds of 55 mph between 1230 and 130 pm MST. The Albuquerque ASOS reported a peak wind gust of 60 mph at 200 pm and sustained winds of 43 mph from 200 pm to 300 pm MST. A peak wind gust of 60 mph was recorded at the top of the Tram at 100 pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few showers, which amounted to sprinkles at best, produced very gusty winds near and north of Clayton.  These dry microbursts resulted in a 58 mph wind gust at the Clayton ASOS.  Sustained winds were only around 12 mph.
The Swallow Fire quickly engulfed 9 homes amidst breezy, hot and very dry conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This human caused fire, named the Swallow Fire for starting on Swallow Drive, burned 10 acres of land in a wooded Ruidoso neighborhood.  Nine homes were lost to the blaze.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very high precipitable water values combined with modest instability allowed storms to develop across much of the state on the 11th.  Strong storms near and over the Wallow Fire burn scar triggered flash flooding downstream near the town of Luna.  The Luna RAWS picked up over one inch of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An unnamed arroyo feeding into the San Francisco River became flooded near the Luna Post Office.  The water was at least four feet high and washed away the porch steps of a nearby mobile home.  The headwaters of the arroyo are located northwest of town, within the Wallow Fire burn scar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions developed across the east central and southeast plains of New Mexico in late February. After an abysmal showing of winter precipitation, much of the plains were less than 25 percent of normal for the December through February period. These conditions are expected to worsen, thus setting the stage for wildfires.  Monetary damages will be estimated at the end of the drought.

Drought in the southwest mountains began in late January of 2011. Winter precipitation was well below normal across the southwest mountains of New Mexico which allowed severe (D2) drought to continue into February.  Percent of normal precipitation ranged from 11 percent to 68 percent across southern parts of the area for the December through February period.  These drought conditions are expected to worsen, setting the stage for wildfires.  Monetary damages will be estimated at the end of the event.

Severe (D2) drought crept northward from the southwest mountains and southeast plains in January and February, to much of central New Mexico in March.  With little or no precipitation in April, combined with above normal temperatures and strong winds, the drought persisted and in many areas worsened.  Most locations along and south of Interstate 40 were pushed into extreme (D3) drought in April.  Calendar year precipitation was less than 20 percent of normal in these areas. State averaged precipitation for January through April was ranked as the 3rd driest of the 117 years since 1895. As these drought conditions continued to worsen, human caused wildfires began to pop up across portions of the state. Monetary damages will be estimated at the end of the event.

An unusually deep upper low moved through the Great Basin with a surface low located in extreme Northeast New Mexico. This setup a tight pressure gradient across South-Central New Mexico with wind gusts up to 71 mph reported at San Augustin Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gust of 71 mph was reported at San Augustin Pass and a 62 mph gust was reported 5 miles northeast of San Augustin Pass.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gust of 68 mph was reported at San Augustin Pass and a gust of 58 mph was also reported 5 miles northeast of San Augustin Pass.

An unseasonably cold late season upper level low pressure system over the northern Rockies developed southward into the Four Corners region on May 1st.  A potent cold front also moved south and west across the eastern plains providing support for strong easterly upslope flow along the central mountain chain. Widespread snowfall between 6 and 10 inches was reported over the northern mountains, with lighter amounts of 2 to 6 inches of the surrounding highlands and upper Rio Grande Valley.  Strong gap winds within the central valley also delivered wind gusts between 40 and 60 mph during the early morning hours May 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various heavy snowfall amounts between 6 and 11 inches reported over the Jemez Mountains.  Wolf Canyon reported 11 inches and Vacas Locas SNOTEL 8 inches.

Five major winter storms in December put a dent in the drought across New Mexico. Most areas received well above normal precipitation for the month.  In fact, many areas received record or near record precipitation totals for the month.  One area that missed out was portions of northwestern New Mexico, though these areas have not yet entered drought status.  This is mainly due to the northwest plateau being the wettest area of the state in 2011, receiving 96 percent of normal precipitation.  On the other hand, the southeast plains were the driest area of the state, at about 50 percent of normal.  Statewide average precipitation during 2011 was 73 percent of normal, making 2011 the driest year since 2003 and the sixth driest on record since the mid 1890s.  ||Despite the above normal precipitation in December, much of the state remained in drought status.  Most areas, however, did drop a category. The northeast plains, central valleys and southwest mountains dropped from extreme (D3) drought to severe (D2) drought status. Meanwhile, the exceptional (D4) drought across the southeast dropped to extreme (D3) drought status. Northwest areas improved as well, and only the eastern portions of the San Juan Mountains, west central mountains and west central highlands remain in severe (D2) drought status. Monetary damages will be estimated at the end of the event.

A powerful cold front plunged south and west across the eastern plains of New Mexico on the 1st and spilled over the top of the Central Mountains into the Rio Grande Valley. Wind gusts between 60 and 90 mph caused widespread damage to roofs and power lines around Albuquerque, Socorro and even Grants.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A truck rolled over on U.S. Route 64/87 between Capulin and Raton due to high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two successive upper level storm systems effectively brought a one-two punch to New Mexico.  Winter weather began on the 31st of January when an arctic cold front blasted through the eastern plains.  Snow began that evening in earnest across the eastern plains and central mountain chain.  Rain also changed to snow across the west as temperatures cooled. Snow continued into the 2nd, which was then followed by extremely cold temperatures.  Snow totals exceeded one foot in many mountain locales, while somewhat lower amounts were noted below 7500 feet.  This event continued into early February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 4 and 5 inches of snow fell around Gallup, with lesser amounts further south.  This event continued into February.

Wind gusts between 60 and 90 mph caused widespread damage to roofs and power lines around Albuquerque, Socorro and even Grants.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds between 40 and 55 mph and gusts between 60 and 90 mph were common across the Albuquerque Metro Area with numerous reports of roof damage, downed power lines, evaporative coolers blown off roofs, tree limbs snapped and trees toppled over. Over 2500 damage claims have been filed in and around the Albuquerque Metro area with damage in the millions. The winds also took on the University of New Mexico football practice facility, with damage noted to the south walls and roof. Also, the Valencia High School roof in Los Lunas was partially damaged from high winds.  This damage alone cost an estimated $15,000.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two commercial buildings, one home and two motels sustained roof damage from high winds around Grants. In addition, one business sustained minor roof damage in Milan.  Numerous trees were toppled and limbs were blown down.

An upper level cutoff low pressure center moved slowly eastward over west Texas, creating a prolonged period of wintry precipitation.  Although rain, sleet and snow occurred, the precipitation fell mainly as snow with significant accumulations over southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals were great enough by 714 PM CST on the 23rd over east central New Mexico for Highway 18 to be closed between Hobbs and Lovington.  There were injuries from several automobile accidents reported.   US Highway 62/180 between Hobbs and Carlsbad was closed by 825 PM CST.  Law enforcement was working an accident on US 82 west of Lovington at that time also.  Total snowfall at Hobbs was 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level cutoff low pressure center moved slowly eastward over west Texas, creating a prolonged period of wintry precipitation.  Although rain, sleet and snow occurred, the precipitation fell mainly as snow with significant accumulations over southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was only 1-2 inches by early evening on the 23rd, however roads began to be closed over Lea County due to snowfall and subfreezing temperatures.  The storm total snowfall reported for Tatum was 8 inches.

A powerful cold front plunged south and west across the eastern plains of New Mexico on the 1st and spilled over the top of the Central Mountains into the Rio Grande Valley. Wind gusts between 60 and 90 mph caused widespread damage to roofs and power lines around Albuquerque, Socorro and even Grants.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds caused damage for several hours across the lower Rio Grande Valley. Pro panel roofing was blown off the Chaves Community Center near the community of La Nutrias on Highway 304 around 2 pm LST. A bit later, a roof blew off a hay barn at the Black and White Dairy in Abeytas, NM west of mile marker 4 on Highway 116. Also, a mobile home on Wallner Road in the Ranchos de Veguita subdivision was blown apart and scattered approximately 300 feet from where it was sitting around 5 pm LST.  No one was home during the incident.  Around the same time, a mobile home was turned on its side near the Veguita Trading Post. Around 11 pm, a large tree was blown down across Highway 304 at mile marker 11.  Several other mobile homes were damaged in northern Socorro County by Able Wash due to the winds, and one fire engine was damaged east of Bernardo.  The strong winds caused sand in the area to pile up on area roadways.  Approximately one foot of sand was noted on the roads west of Bernardo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After the first winter storm earlier in the month, portions of southwest New Mexico received light snow accumulations with a second upper level system, but it was the third system that brought another round of widespread heavy snow. Another back door cold front moved down the plains on the 4th and westward through the gaps of the central mountain chain.  Snow developed along the front, including in central New Mexico, and continued through the early afternoon hours of the 5th before the upper level system moved eastward. Unlike the first winter storm, however, some of the heaviest snows were found across central portions of the state, even portions of Rio Rancho. Even Interstate 25 was closed south of Belen due to treacherous driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near Dulce, between 2 and 5 inches of snow was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level cutoff low pressure center moved slowly eastward over west Texas, creating a prolonged period of wintry precipitation.  Although rain, sleet and snow occurred, the precipitation fell mainly as snow with significant accumulations over southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall mixed with sleet late on the 22nd until early on the 23rd, before changing to all snow.  Snowfall of 3+ inches was reported in Hope at 735 AM CST on the 23rd along with icy roads.  The final snow total at Hope was 12 inches reported at 922 AM CST on the 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level cutoff low pressure center moved slowly eastward over west Texas, creating a prolonged period of wintry precipitation.  Although rain, sleet and snow occurred, the precipitation fell mainly as snow with significant accumulations over southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the eveing of the 22nd, and fell through the night with 3-4 inches reported on the ground in Carlsbad the morning of December 23rd.  The temperature was in the upper 20s.  Snowfall continued nearly unabated through the day on the 23rd and 24th with a storm total of 12-14 inches.  The Carlsbad ASOS (KCNM) had the snow beginning at roughly the same time the storm spotters reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level cutoff low pressure center moved slowly eastward over west Texas, creating a prolonged period of wintry precipitation.  Although rain, sleet and snow occurred, the precipitation fell mainly as snow with significant accumulations over southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads became snow packed and icy around Artesia by 835 AM CST on the 23rd, although snowfall totals were only 1-2 inches.  Snowfall persisted through the day and night with Artesia ending up with a total of 9.5 inches which was reported at 11:16 AM CST on the 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two successive upper level storm systems effectively brought a one-two punch to New Mexico.  Winter weather began on the 31st of January when an arctic cold front blasted through the eastern plains.  Snow began that evening in earnest across the eastern plains and central mountain chain.  Rain also changed to snow across the west as temperatures cooled. Snow continued into the 2nd, which was then followed by extremely cold temperatures.  Snow totals exceeded one foot in many mountain locales, while somewhat lower amounts were noted below 7500 feet.  This event continued into early February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Vacas Locas SNOTEL reported about 8 inches of snow. This event continued into February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong lee-side surface low combined with strong mid level winds mixing down to the surface resulted in severe wind gusts across much of the east central plains and the high peaks of the southwest mountains. Visibilities were reduced to near zero in blowing dust around Roswell and other portions of the east central plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Magdalena Ridge Observatory local weather station recorded severe wind gusts for much of the day.  The peak wind gust of 92 mph was observed around 9am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low dropped down the Northern Rockies which pushed a back door arctic cold front through the region. An upper level ridge was located over the eastern Pacific with the arctic jet steam diving south to the Mexican border.  These two features combined to bring extremely cold air into the Western United States from Alaska and Canada. Numerous areas experienced rolling power outages and natural gas shortages along with burst pipes. The state of New Mexico declared a State of Emergency due to the natural gas shortages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures on Feb 2nd only warmed to around 19F at Silver City and 23 at the GIla Center RAWS. Low temperatures on the morning of Feb 3rd dropped to -9F at the Silver City Airport, -13F at Mule Creek, and -11F at the Gila Center RAWS.  Natural gas was shut off to many residents around the Silver City area because of low pressure in the lines due to the high demand.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low dropped down the Northern Rockies which pushed a back door arctic cold front through the region. An upper level ridge was located over the eastern Pacific with the arctic jet steam diving south to the Mexican border.  These two features combined to bring extremely cold air into the Western United States from Alaska and Canada. Numerous areas experienced rolling power outages and natural gas shortages along with burst pipes. The state of New Mexico declared a State of Emergency due to the natural gas shortages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures across the region on Feb 2nd included 16F at Deming and 18F about 10 miles south of Deming. Low temperatures on the morning of Feb 3rd included -3F at Cambray near the Luna/Dona Ana County line on Interstate 10, -1F about 10 miles south of Deming, and 0F at Deming. Numerous frozen and burst water pipes were reported.  The Marshall Memorial Library in Deming was closed for some time due to a break in the building's fire suppression system causing damage to the ceiling and insulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong lee-side surface low combined with strong mid level winds mixing down to the surface resulted in severe wind gusts across much of the east central plains and the high peaks of the southwest mountains.  These strong winds began during the early morning hours, and lasted much of the afternoon.  Visibilities were reduced to near zero in blowing dust around Roswell and other portions of the east central plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Texas Mesonet Station southwest of Dora recorded wind gusts up to 64 mph through the late morning and early afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Five major winter storms in December put a dent in the drought across New Mexico. Most areas received well above normal precipitation for the month.  In fact, many areas received record or near record precipitation totals for the month.  One area that missed out was portions of northwestern New Mexico, though these areas have not yet entered drought status.  This is mainly due to the northwest plateau being the wettest area of the state in 2011, receiving 96 percent of normal precipitation.  On the other hand, the southeast plains were the driest area of the state, at about 50 percent of normal.  Statewide average precipitation during 2011 was 73 percent of normal, making 2011 the driest year since 2003 and the sixth driest on record since the mid 1890s.  ||Despite the above normal precipitation in December, much of the state remained in drought status.  Most areas, however, did drop a category. The northeast plains, central valleys and southwest mountains dropped from extreme (D3) drought to severe (D2) drought status. Meanwhile, the exceptional (D4) drought across the southeast dropped to extreme (D3) drought status. Northwest areas improved as well, and only the eastern portions of the San Juan Mountains, west central mountains and west central highlands remain in severe (D2) drought status. Monetary damages will be estimated at the end of the event.

After the first winter storm earlier in the month, portions of southwest New Mexico received light snow accumulations with a second upper level system, but it was the third system that brought another round of widespread heavy snow. Another back door cold front moved down the plains on the 4th and westward through the gaps of the central mountain chain.  Snow developed along the front, including in central New Mexico, and continued through the early afternoon hours of the 5th before the upper level system moved eastward. Unlike the first winter storm, however, some of the heaviest snows were found across central portions of the state, even portions of Rio Rancho. Even Interstate 25 was closed south of Belen due to treacherous driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of snow was reported around Santa Rosa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper level storm system approached the state from the west-southwest, a band of precipitation formed across central and western New Mexico on the 12th.  This band slowly shifted eastward through the 13th, however at least two more rounds of precipitation moved from southwest to northeast across the area as the main upper level low crossed the state.  Snow levels were initially high, thus, areas above 7500 feet fared well for snowfall.  Many ski areas saw over a foot of fresh snow, allowing them to open the following weekend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately 9 inches of snow was reported at the Rice Park SNOTEL.

A powerful storm and arctic cold front combined to bring fresh snow cover to nearly all of northern and central New Mexico on the 1st and 2nd, as well as extremely cold, record setting minimum temperatures. This resulted in dangerously low wind chill values over many areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures as low as -19 and winds of 5 to 10 mph created wind chill values between -20 to -35 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two successive upper level storm systems effectively brought a one-two punch to New Mexico.  Winter weather began on the 31st of January when an arctic cold front blasted through the eastern plains.  Snow began that evening in earnest across the eastern plains and central mountain chain.  Rain also changed to snow across the west as temperatures cooled. Snow continued into the 2nd, which was then followed by extremely cold temperatures.  Snow totals exceeded one foot in many mountain locales, while somewhat lower amounts were noted below 7500 feet.  This event continued into early February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to 8 inches of snow was common across the central highlands. This event continued into February.

Below zero temperatures in Farmington led to the death of a 47 year old woman.  The woman was a transient who was living in a tent in an area located approximately one mile off East Main Street across from Memory Gardens.

As an upper level storm system approached the state from the west-southwest, a band of precipitation formed across central and western New Mexico on the 12th.  This band slowly shifted eastward through the 13th, however at least two more rounds of precipitation moved from southwest to northeast across the area as the main upper level low crossed the state.  Snow levels were initially high, thus, areas above 7500 feet fared well for snowfall.  Many ski areas saw over a foot of fresh snow, allowing them to open the following weekend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Elevations above 8000 feet received over a foot of snow, while lower elevations received between 4 and 6 inches.

A strong upper low dropped down the Northern Rockies which pushed a back door arctic cold front through the region. An upper level ridge was located over the eastern Pacific with the arctic jet steam diving south to the Mexican border.  These two features combined to bring extremely cold air into the Western United States from Alaska and Canada. Numerous areas experienced rolling power outages and natural gas shortages along with burst pipes. The state of New Mexico declared a State of Emergency due to the natural gas shortages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures across the Sacramento Mountains on Feb 2nd included -4F at Weed, -3F at Mayhill, -1F in Cloudcroft and 1F at Mescalero. Overnight low temperatures on Feb 3rd included -30F at the Inn of the Mountain Gods in Mescalero, -19F in Cloudcroft, -20F at Mayhill, -16F in Weed and -18F at Cosmic. The town of Cloudcroft lost between 500,000 and 600,000 gallons of water due to water line breaks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system moved across southern New Mexico, and initially brought heavy snow to western and central New Mexico.  Then, a very strong cold front plowed across the northeast and east central plains bringing winds up to 60 mph and more heavy snow. More than a foot of snow fell across Union County.  This caused drifts to be more than 6 feet high in some locations, which stranded holiday travelers, and in at least one case, buried them.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Interstate 25 corridor was hit hard with nearly 15 inches of snow in Wagon Mound.  7 inches of snow was also reported around Abbott. The Raton ASOS measured a gust of 60 mph during the event. Sustained winds of 35 to 45 mph caused substantial blowing and drifting snow.  Snow drifts were over 4 feet.  A horse trailer going southbound overturned on Interstate 25 between Wagon Mound and Springer.  Three people were injured.

Northwest areas improved as well, and only the eastern portions of the San Juan Mountains, west central mountains and west central highlands remain in severe (D2) drought status. Monetary damages will be estimated at the end of the event.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fresh snow cover allowed for temperatures to rapidly drop over northern New Mexico during the late evening of the 8th and early morning hours of the 9th. Low temperature readings exceeded 35 degrees below zero in the Moreno Valley, and temperatures in the negative teens combined with 10 to 15 mph winds resulted in very cold wind chills around Las Vegas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The low temperature at Angel Fire plummeted to 35 degrees below zero.  Winds were generally calm, though brief winds of 5 mph or so dropped the wind chills around -50 degrees. An unofficial observation station in Eagle Nest reported a low temperature of -41 degrees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The low temperature at Las Vegas dropped to 16 degrees below zero.  North winds of 10 to 15 mph dropped wind chills between -25 and-40 degrees through the early morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper level low pressure system over the Great Basin combined with a deep surface low pressure center over eastern Colorado produced widespread strong winds across New Mexico.  The strongest winds developed over the west central plateau and western mountains as well as along and east of much of the central mountain chain, surrounding highlands, and adjacent plains.  Numerous reports of gusts between 55 and 63 mph were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Columbia Scientific Balloon Facility's Transwestern Pump Station northeast of Encinoso recorded a peak wind gust of 61 mph at 115 pm MST, though a 58 mph gust was recorded as early as 1130 am MST.
